s: Bombed by the Allies in 1945, rebuilt from ash within a year
w: Founded 1738 by Fujianese settlers who carried a piece of the Jinjiang Lungshan Temple across the strait. On 31 May 1945, Allied bombers hit the main hall, reportedly targeting Japanese munitions stored inside; the Guanyin statue was found miraculously undamaged amid the rubble. Rebuilt yet again. Never closes.
_facts: Built 1738 by settlers from Fujian (Quanzhou) during Qing rule, dedicated to Guanyin
Unusually syncretic: houses Mazu, Guan Yu and other folk deities alongside Buddhist figures
Hit by Allied bombing on 31 May 1945 (Taihoku Air Raid); main hall and left corridor destroyed
Guanyin statue reportedly found intact amid the bombing wreckage
Has been rebuilt multiple times after earthquakes, fires, and wartime damage
Designated a national monument; stays open 24 hours

s: Octagonal market, then theater; now Taipei's LGBT heartland
w: Built 1908 as Taiwan's first public market — an octagonal brick hall that failed as a bazaar and was swiftly repurposed as a theater. Under the KMT it became a cinema, then fell derelict. When the city rescued it in the 1990s, the square behind it became Taipei's main outdoor LGBT gathering strip. The octagon still lords over it all.
_facts: Built 1908 as Taiwan's first public market under Japanese colonial rule
Octagonal design; repurposed as a theater almost immediately after failing as a market
Used as cinema and theater under KMT era; fell into disrepair
Rescued and restored in 1990s as Red House Theater arts venue
Surrounding plaza (Ximen 6th Park) became Taipei's main LGBT social hub
Location: 10 Chengdu Road, Ximending, Wanhua

s: Japan signed its Taiwan surrender here, October 1945
w: Built 1932-1936 as Taihoku City Public Auditorium — one of the four largest in Japan at the time, clad in green tile so Allied bombers would miss it. On 25 October 1945, General Chen Yi accepted Japan's formal Taiwan surrender here (Retrocession Day). Renamed for Sun Yat-sen, it has hosted foreign leaders from Nixon to Ngo Dinh Diem ever since.
_facts: Built 1932-1936 as Taihoku City Public Auditorium, fourth-largest public hall in Japan at the time
Faced in light green tile to reduce visibility to Allied aerial bombers
Japan's formal Taiwan surrender accepted here by Chen Yi on 25 October 1945 (Retrocession Day)
Renamed Zhongshan Hall in honor of Sun Yat-sen after handover to ROC
Designed by Japanese architect Ide Kaoru; cost 980,000 yen
Guests included US President Nixon, South Korean President Rhee, South Vietnam's Ngo Dinh Diem

s: Taiwan's busiest Taoist temple — banned incense to cut air pollution
w: Devoted to Guan Yu — god of war, business and righteous brotherhood — this 1967 temple draws hundreds of thousands of merchants per year. In 2014 it became the first temple in Taiwan to ban indoor incense-burning over particulate pollution. Attendance barely dipped. Fortune-tellers work the street outside.
_facts: Dedicated to Guan Yu (Lord Guan), patron deity of businessmen, military and police
Built 1967 in Zhongshan District, covers over 7,000 square metres
In 2014 became first temple in Taiwan to ban the burning of incense due to air pollution concerns
Hundreds of volunteer fortune interpreters (bua poe) operate near the temple
Among the most visited temples in Taiwan by annual worshipper count

s: Democracy dissidents plotted here; Ang Lee filmed here too
w: A Japanese wooden house from the 1920s named for the wisteria that canopies its entrance. In the martial-law 1980s it became the city's most dangerous living room: democracy advocates gathered here to resist the KMT. Designated a heritage monument in 1997. Ang Lee filmed here for 'Eat Drink Man Woman' (1994).
_facts: Japanese-style wooden house built in the 1920s on Xinsheng South Road, Da'an District
Named for three wisteria vines planted in the front courtyard
From 1981, became a meeting place for democratic dissidents during Taiwan's martial law era
Figures including Lei Chen and economist Chou Te-wei used it as a gathering place
Designated a historic monument by Taipei City Government in 1997
Used as a filming location in Ang Lee's 'Eat Drink Man Woman' (1994)

s: Taiwan's oldest mosque, funded by the Shah of Iran and King of Jordan
w: Taiwan's Muslim community built their first prayer space in 1948 — a converted Japanese house. By 1960 that was too small; the Shah of Iran and the King of Jordan contributed to construction costs, the Continental Engineering Corporation built it, and the ROC Vice President cut the ribbon. Architect Yang Cho-cheng gave it twin minarets and neo-Byzantine domes. Largest and oldest mosque in Taiwan.
_facts: First mosque in Taiwan opened August 1948 in a converted Japanese house on Lishui Street
Current building completed April 13, 1960 on Xinsheng South Road, Da'an
Designed by architect Yang Cho-cheng in Islamic/Neo-Byzantine style
Construction funded partly by the Shah of Iran and King of Jordan
Built by Continental Engineering Corporation; inaugurated by ROC Vice President Chen Cheng
Capacity 1,000 worshippers; two minarets 20m high

s: Taiwan's oldest museum, opened 1908 to celebrate 10 years of Japanese rule
w: Opened on 24 October 1908 to mark the inauguration of the North-South Railway — and, with grim colonial tidiness, Japan's first decade of rule over Taiwan. The Neoclassical building inside 228 Peace Memorial Park is the only museum on the island still operating on its original colonial site. Natural history, ethnography and indigenous artefacts from day one. National Heritage since 1998.
_facts: Officially opened 24 October 1908 as Taiwan Governor's Museum
Opened to mark the North-South Railway inauguration and ten years of Japanese colonial rule
Current building in 228 Peace Memorial Park inaugurated 1915
Only museum in Taiwan established during colonial years still on its original site
Declared National Heritage by Ministry of Interior in June 1998
Founded with over 10,000 natural history and ethnographic items

s: Sole surviving Qing city gate in its original form, built 1884
w: Of the five gates the Qing dynasty built around Taipei in 1884, four were 'restored' — which meant the originals were replaced by Chinese-revival concrete. This one, Cheng'en Gate, was saved by the elevated expressway that hemmed it in for decades. When the freeway came down in 2016, the two-storey brick blockhouse with its curved grey-tile roof stood revealed: the only intact glimpse of what the Qing wall actually looked like.
_facts: Constructed 1884 as one of five gates of the Qing-era walls of Taipei
Formally named Cheng'en Gate (Gate of Bearing Grace)
Best-preserved of Taipei's historic city gates; four others were rebuilt losing original structure
Surrounded by elevated freeway for decades; freeway demolished 2016, restoring visual prominence
Two-story closed blockhouse of solid brick with traditional Chinese wooden roof truss
National Monument of Taiwan

s: From sake brewery to underground arts; squatters saved it in 1997
w: The Japanese opened this 19,800 sq-metre winery in 1914; it later produced cheap cassava liquor that became a working-class staple. The distillery closed in 1987 and sat derelict until 1997, when the Golden Bough Theatre Group broke in and staged a performance in the ruins — sparking a preservation campaign that turned it into Taipei's main alternative arts venue. The corrugated-iron machine halls and boiler stacks are all still there.
_facts: Opened 1914 as Taihoku Winery under Japanese rule, producing sake and ginseng wine
Taken over by Taiwan Governor-General's Monopoly Bureau in 1922
Produced 'Taibai Liquor' from cassava from 1949 — a cheap working-class staple
Winery closed 1987 due to rapid urbanization
In 1997 Golden Bough Theatre Group staged an illegal performance here, catalysing preservation
Reopened as Huashan 1914 Creative Park arts venue in 2005

s: A 1937 Japanese tobacco plant now hosting Taiwan's design scene
w: Built in 1937 as the Matsuyama Tobacco Plant — the Governor-General marked its opening as a celebration of Japan's forty years of colonial rule. Workers rolled cigarettes conveyed by belt directly into the warehouses. Production ended in 1998; the complex became Taipei's 99th historic site in 2001. The Baroque garden, boiler room and nursery building survive intact among the warehouses.
_facts: Built 1937 as Matsuyama Tobacco Plant under Japanese colonial government
Opening celebrated as marking 40 years of Japanese rule of Taiwan
Workers manufactured cigarettes on second floor; conveyed to warehouses via belt and turntable
Ceased production 1998
Designated Taipei's 99th historic site in 2001 by Taipei City Government
Converted to Songshan Cultural and Creative Park in 2011

s: The Governor-General's baroque palace; Emperor Hirohito slept here
w: Built 1899-1901 as the official residence of the Governor-General of Taiwan, this French Baroque confection caused scandal in Tokyo: budget for the Taiwan Grand Shrine was reportedly diverted to fund it. Crown Prince Hirohito stayed here during his 1923 Taiwan visit. After retrocession it became a ROC state guest house for Nixon and others. Open to the public on certain dates only.
_facts: Built 1899-1901, designed by Japanese architects Dogo Fukuda and Ichiro Nomura
Rebuilt/expanded 1911 by Moriyama; construction scandal over diverted Shinto shrine funds
Served as Governor-General's official residence until 1920
Crown Prince Hirohito (later Emperor Showa) stayed here during Taiwan visit
Became ROC state guest house after 1945 retrocession; hosted Nixon and other dignitaries
Declared national heritage site July 1998

s: Former horse track turned White Terror killing field, 1950-1992
w: The Japanese used this riverbank site as a military training ground; it briefly served as Taipei South Airport before closure in 1949. Under Chiang Kai-shek's government it became the principal execution ground of the White Terror — the KMT's campaign of political repression that ran from the February 28 Massacre through the end of martial law and beyond. Political prisoners were brought here to be shot. Quiet, riverbank-haunted. Declared a historic site in 2020.
_facts: Originally Japanese military training ground (Babachō); briefly a small airfield until 1949
Became principal execution ground for political prisoners during the White Terror
White Terror: martial law ran 1949-1987; KMT repression began with Feb 28 1947 massacre
Notable prisoners executed here included Wu Shi and other political dissidents
Park established 2000 under mayor Chen Shui-bian; named by next mayor Ma Ying-jeou
Declared a historic site by Taipei City Government on 6 March 2020

s: A fake palace on a real Shinto shrine; tallest Chinese-style building alive
w: Chiang Kai-shek wanted a hotel to impress visitors; his wife suggested the hill where Japan's Taiwan Grand Shrine had stood. Architect Yang Cho-cheng delivered a 12-storey imperial palace in vermilion and jade. A 1995 roof fire — ladders couldn't reach — afterwards prompted the two rooftop dragon heads to be rotated inward to 'face water'. Hidden 168-metre escape slide in the hollow pillars. Tallest Chinese classical-style building in the world.
_facts: Main building completed 10 October 1973 (National Day); established May 1952
Built on the site of Taiwan Grand Shrine, Japan's most important Shinto complex on the island
Designed by Yang Cho-cheng in Chinese palace style on Chiang Kai-shek's instruction
87 metres tall, 12 stories; claimed as tallest Chinese classical-style building in the world
Named one of world's top 10 hotels by US Fortune magazine in 1968
1995 fire destroyed roof — ladders couldn't reach; rooftop dragon heads rotated 180 degrees inward afterward
Hidden 168-metre emergency slide concealed in the hollow foundation pillars

n: Shung Ye Museum of Formosan Aborigines
s: Only private museum for Taiwan's 16 indigenous Austronesian peoples
w: Opened June 1994 by the Shung Ye Group, 200 metres from the National Palace Museum but concerning entirely different millennia of Taiwan's history: the island's 16 officially recognised indigenous peoples, all Austronesian-speaking, all here before the Han migrations of the 17th century. Weaving, ceramics, hunting tools, ritual dress. A quiet corrective to the Palace across the road.
_facts: Opened June 1994 as Taiwan's first private museum dedicated to Taiwanese indigenous peoples
Located in Shilin District, approximately 200 metres from National Palace Museum
Dedicated to the culture and history of all 16 officially recognised indigenous tribes of Taiwan
All indigenous peoples of Taiwan are Austronesian-language speakers
Houses permanent and rotating exhibitions of artefacts from mountain and coastal tribes
Funded and established by Shung Ye Group

s: Six Nobel nominations; the man who gave the West 'My Country and My People'
w: Lin Yutang — prolific bilingual writer, six-time Nobel Literature nominee — designed this blue-tiled courtyard house himself in 1966, blending traditional Chinese and Spanish elements. He lived and wrote here until his death in 1976. The small library preserves his papers and the patents for his Chinese typewriter (US patent 1952) — a beautiful device that encoded characters on a rotating drum and was never commercially produced.
_facts: Lin Yutang (1895-1976): prolific bilingual writer in Chinese and English, nominated six times for Nobel Prize in Literature
Best known works: 'My Country and My People' (1935) and 'The Importance of Living' (1937)
Invented a Chinese typewriter patented in the United States in 1952 — never mass-produced
House built in 1966, designed by Lin himself combining traditional Chinese courtyard and Spanish elements
Blue tiles and white walls; central courtyard layout
Lin died here in 1976; house opened as museum by Taipei City Government

s: Japan's largest Taiwan workshop; 16 hectares of machinery, silent since 2012
w: Built in 1935 as the centrepiece of a colonial exhibition marking Japan's 40th year of rule, this 16.82-hectare complex was the largest railway workshop ever built in Taiwan — a 168-metre train-assembly shed at its heart. For decades it overhauled every train on the network. It closed in 2012, was declared a national historic site in 2015, and is slowly becoming a railway museum.
_facts: Built 1935 as largest railway workshop in Taiwan, replacing the older workshop
Established as part of celebrations for Japan's 40th anniversary of ruling Taiwan
16.82-hectare site on Civic Boulevard, Xinyi District; included 168-metre-long train assembly shed
Operated by Taiwan Railways Administration; maintained thousands of railway vehicles over decades
Closed in 2012 when operations transferred to TRA Fugang Vehicle Depot
Declared official national historic site March 2015; planned as future railway museum
